heuristics.rule: Do not disclose visa/sponsorship status before the company has decided they want you
heuristics.condition: When applying or in early interviews as an international student requiring sponsorship...
heuristics.action: ...avoid proactively mentioning OPT/H-1B status unless explicitly asked; research sponsorship capacity through H-1B data and alumni intelligence instead
heuristics.because: Sponsorship cost becomes an elimination filter before your qualifications are weighed — disclosing early raises costs before benefits are established

heuristics.rule: Negotiate salary using market data, never personal need or visa-adjusted pricing
heuristics.condition: When countering an offer as a visa holder...
heuristics.action: ...ground the counter in levels.fyi, H-1B salary data, and peer compensation ranges rather than personal financial needs or immigration constraints
heuristics.because: Disclosing your reservation price or framing requests around personal need reveals your floor and eliminates negotiating room

heuristics.rule: A higher salary negotiation is simultaneously a better H-1B lottery outcome post-2025
heuristics.condition: When negotiating compensation under the wage-weighted H-1B lottery system...
heuristics.action: ...explicitly frame targeting a Level III or IV salary as benefiting both parties — higher comp for you, better lottery odds for the company's investment
heuristics.because: Salary and immigration interests are directly aligned under wage-weighted lottery rules, making this a rare non-zero-sum negotiation argument

heuristics.rule: Reframe visa dependency as a retention differentiator, not a cost
heuristics.condition: When an employer treats sponsorship as a burden during comp discussions...
heuristics.action: ...explicitly state that H-1B holders stay 2–3 years minimum due to switching complexity, making them a long-term retention asset that justifies the salary requested
heuristics.because: This flips the power dynamic from 'I'm a cost because of my visa' to 'I'm a retention asset,' neutralizing the employer's implicit leverage

heuristics.rule: Verify sponsorship execution capacity before signing, not just sponsorship willingness
heuristics.condition: When a company says they will sponsor H-1B but you have no confirmation...
heuristics.action: ...ask explicitly: do they have immigration counsel, have they sponsored before, what is their approval rate, who is currently on H-1B there, and get written confirmation before accepting
heuristics.because: Sponsorship willingness and execution capacity are different risks — a company without immigration infrastructure is a material risk regardless of intent

heuristics.rule: Sponsorship cost does not justify below-market comp
heuristics.condition: When an employer implies your salary should be lower because of sponsorship overhead...
heuristics.action: ...counter that sponsorship attorney and USCIS fees total $3,000–$8,000/year, which is less than 5% of a $150,000+ employee's total cost — not a justification for 20% below-market pay
heuristics.because: The actual cost of sponsorship is small relative to total compensation; treating it as a salary discount is a negotiation tactic, not a financial reality

heuristics.rule: Know H-1B portability rights to make competing threats credible
heuristics.condition: When feeling trapped at a current employer due to visa dependency...
heuristics.action: ...internalize that H-1B portability allows switching employers without waiting for approval if the new petition is filed within 60 days, making the threat to leave or imply alternatives genuinely credible
heuristics.because: Perceived lock-in is the source of employer leverage; knowing your legal rights converts a structural disadvantage into a negotiable constraint

decision_factors.factor: Company's H-1B sponsorship history and approval rate
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A company with no prior H-1B sponsorships or low approval rates represents a material risk that the offer is not executable, regardless of the salary number
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check public USCIS H-1B disclosure data, ask directly whether they have immigration counsel and current H-1B employees, and request confirmation in writing before signing

decision_factors.factor: OEWS wage level classification of the offer
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Post-2025, wage level directly determines H-1B lottery odds — Level III or IV gives significantly better odds, meaning the wage level affects both comp and immigration outcomes simultaneously
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Cross-reference the offered salary against OEWS wage tables for the job's SOC code and geographic location to determine which level it falls into

decision_factors.factor: OPT expiration date and H-1B filing deadline
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Timeline urgency changes negotiation posture — a student with 6 months of OPT remaining has more flexibility than one with 6 weeks, affecting BATNA credibility and process parallelism
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate exact OPT end date, STEM OPT extension eligibility, and the H-1B cap filing window (April 1 each year) to map your actual runway

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Without a real alternative — whether a competing offer, another degree program, CPT position, or another country's job market — counter-offers lack credibility and the employer retains leverage
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify at least one path you would genuinely pursue if this offer fell through; a BATNA only works if you would actually use it

decision_factors.factor: Whether the company has decided they want you before visa status is disclosed
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Leverage shifts dramatically once a company is invested in hiring you — sponsorship cost is weighed against losing a desired candidate, not against an unknown applicant
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Track whether you've received a verbal or written signal of intent to hire before any sponsorship conversation begins

decision_factors.factor: Market compensation data for the role and level
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Negotiating from objective market data removes the need to disclose personal need and establishes an external benchmark that is harder to dismiss than personal requests
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Use levels.fyi, H-1B salary disclosure data for the specific company and role, and peer data to establish a credible range before negotiating

decision_factors.factor: Whether the student is negotiating from fear or from data
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Fear-based negotiation leads to accepting the first offer and systematically under-negotiating by 10–30% compared to domestic peers, compounding to hundreds of thousands of dollars over a career
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask yourself: is my counter grounded in a salary range from public data, or am I asking for what feels safe given my visa anxiety?

decision_factors.factor: Whether immigration commitment is confirmed in writing
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Verbal sponsorship promises are unenforceable; without written confirmation, the offer may be real on comp but not real on immigration
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Request explicit written confirmation of H-1B filing intent as part of the offer package before signing

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting the first offer to avoid friction and preserve the sponsorship relationship
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically under-negotiating by 10–30% relative to domestic peers, which compounds over a career to hundreds of thousands of dollars in lost compensation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students on OPT and early H-1B holders who conflate sponsorship preservation with salary acceptance

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Disclosing visa status early in the application process to be transparent
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Sponsorship cost becomes a filter that eliminates you before your qualifications are assessed — you lose the evaluation before the competition begins
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who interpret transparency as an early-stage obligation rather than a strategic timing decision

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating H-1B dependency as a permanent lock-in that prevents job switching
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Employers who understand that you believe you cannot leave will price your comp accordingly — the psychological trap becomes a real financial one even if the legal constraint is softer than believed
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: H-1B holders who don't know about the 60-day portability rule and therefore cannot credibly imply alternatives

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a sponsorship commitment without verifying execution infrastructure
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You may sign and onboard only to discover the company has no immigration counsel, has never completed an H-1B, and cannot actually deliver the sponsorship — leaving you in status limbo
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who distinguish sponsorship willingness from execution capacity too late — after accepting the offer

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Framing long-term visa commitment as a weakness during negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You surrender a genuine retention differentiator — the fact that you are likely to stay 2–3 years longer than domestic peers — and allow the employer to use it as leverage instead of you using it as value
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who have internalized the visa constraint as purely negative and haven't been exposed to the reframe

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Running a single-company job search to preserve OPT time
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You eliminate competing offer leverage entirely, capping your negotiating ceiling at whatever the single company is willing to offer without external market pressure
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: OPT students who are risk-averse about timeline and underestimate the financial return of a multi-process search

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting the first offer out of fear that any counter will cost the sponsorship
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Visa dependency creates asymmetric loss aversion — the perceived downside of losing sponsorship feels catastrophic, overriding the upside calculus of negotiating; fear is calibrated to the wrong level of risk
failure_patterns.consequence: 10–30% systematic under-compensation relative to domestic peers; compounded over a career, this is hundreds of thousands of dollars in foregone earnings
warning_signs: Thinking 'I should just be grateful they're willing to sponsor me'
Accepting without asking any questions about comp flexibility

failure_patterns.pattern: Disclosing visa status before the company is invested in hiring you
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate honesty with timing — believing transparency requires early disclosure, or they fear being 'caught' later if they wait
failure_patterns.consequence: Sponsorship cost becomes an elimination filter before qualifications are weighed, reducing interview opportunities unnecessarily
warning_signs: Mentioning OPT or H-1B need in a cover letter unprompted
Volunteering visa status in an initial phone screen before being asked

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating from personal financial need rather than market data
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students anchor to what they need to survive (student loan payments, living costs) rather than what the market pays, because personal need feels more concrete and urgent
failure_patterns.consequence: Reveals the negotiating floor to the employer, who can then anchor just above it — eliminating all upside from the range
warning_signs: Framing a counter as 'I need at least X because of my loans'
Not having looked up compensation data on levels.fyi or H-1B disclosure databases before negotiating

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting 'we'll figure out sponsorship' as a sufficient commitment before signing
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are relieved to hear a positive sponsorship signal and don't want to seem demanding by pressing for specifics, especially after salary negotiation has already created perceived friction
failure_patterns.consequence: Onboarding into a company that lacks immigration infrastructure — no counsel, no prior H-1B experience — and facing a failed or delayed H-1B filing with status consequences
warning_signs: No written confirmation of H-1B filing intent in the offer documentation
Company cannot name their immigration attorney when asked

failure_patterns.pattern: Believing H-1B dependency means you cannot credibly imply you might leave
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students overestimate the legal and logistical complexity of H-1B transfer and underestimate their rights under portability rules, making employer leverage feel absolute when it is not
failure_patterns.consequence: Surrenders all implied BATNA power in negotiations, allowing employers to make take-it-or-leave-it offers without credible pushback
warning_signs: Not researching H-1B portability rules before entering a negotiation
Declining to explore competing offers because 'it's too complicated with my visa'

failure_patterns.pattern: Conflating sponsorship willingness with sponsorship execution capacity
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Both are binary 'yes/no' answers in conversation, making them feel equivalent — students don't know what questions to ask to distinguish intent from capability
failure_patterns.consequence: Accepting an offer from a company that wants to sponsor but cannot execute, resulting in a failed H-1B and potential status loss
warning_signs: Company has never sponsored an H-1B before but says they're willing to start
No current H-1B employees identifiable at the company

diagnostic_questions.question: Has this company sponsored H-1B before, and what is their historical approval rate?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the offer is executable on immigration — a company with no prior H-1B history or low approval rates is a fundamentally different risk than one with a track record
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Who is their immigration counsel, and can you get written confirmation of sponsorship intent in the offer?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do they have immigration counsel retained? Are they willing to commit in writing? If neither, treat the immigration component of the offer as unconfirmed.

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your OPT end date, and when is the next H-1B cap filing window?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: How much time pressure you're actually under — which determines whether you have runway to run a multi-company search or whether you're in a deadline-driven single-offer scenario
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you have 6+ months, you have time for parallel processes; treat this like any other negotiation with competing offers as the goal.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If you're within 90 days of OPT expiration, identify your real BATNA — STEM OPT extension, another degree, CPT, or another country's job market — before entering any negotiation.

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your real BATNA — what would you actually do if this offer fell through?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether your negotiating leverage is genuine or performed — a BATNA only functions if you would credibly pursue it; an unconvincing alternative gives the employer all the power
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you have a competing offer or credible alternative, your leverage is real — use it explicitly and anchor high.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If your BATNA is weak, invest in building one: run parallel processes, identify extension options, or explore other markets before negotiating.

diagnostic_questions.question: What OEWS wage level would the offered salary classify you at under the post-2025 H-1B lottery system?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether there is an alignment between salary negotiation and immigration outcome — a Level I or II offer may be negotiable upward using the mutual lottery odds benefit as leverage
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If Level III or IV, confirm this in your negotiation framing as a shared benefit. If Level I or II, use the wage-weighted lottery argument explicitly to push for Level III classification.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If the company is unaware of the wage-weighted lottery system, educating them may itself open a salary conversation that wasn't available before.

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you anchoring your counter to market data, or to what feels safe given your visa situation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you are negotiating as a rational market participant or as a fear-managed applicant — the answer determines whether your counter has external validity or just reflects anxiety
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If anchored to market data (levels.fyi, H-1B disclosures), your counter is defensible and credible — proceed with confidence.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If anchored to personal comfort or fear, stop and research market comps for your role, level, and company before sending any counter.

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have written confirmation of H-1B sponsorship intent as part of the offer package?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the immigration component of the offer is real or informal — verbal commitments are unenforceable and leave you exposed if the company's circumstances or personnel change
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm it specifies H-1B filing (not just OPT extension), the expected timeline, and names the immigration attorney or firm.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do not sign until you have written confirmation — request it explicitly as a condition of acceptance and treat the absence of it as a yellow flag about execution capacity.

diagnostic_questions.question: Does the company know you're aware of H-1B portability rights and that you have real alternatives?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you have implicitly ceded BATNA power by signaling lock-in — employers who believe you cannot leave will price comp accordingly, even if the legal reality is more flexible
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you've signaled awareness of portability and alternatives, you're negotiating from a stronger position than most visa holders in the same situation.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Consider whether your communication has inadvertently signaled desperation or lock-in — adjust framing to imply optionality without being adversarial.

industry_realities.common_belief: Sponsoring H-1B is expensive and justifies paying an international candidate below market
industry_realities.actual_reality: H-1B sponsorship costs $3,000–$8,000/year in attorney and USCIS fees — less than 5% of a $150,000+ employee's total cost. It is not a meaningful financial justification for 20% below-market compensation.
industry_realities.implication: When an employer uses sponsorship cost as a reason to offer less, it is a negotiation tactic, not a financial reality. Counter with the actual cost figures.

industry_realities.common_belief: Pushing back on an offer as an international student risks having it rescinded, especially with sponsorship at stake
industry_realities.actual_reality: Offers are almost never rescinded for reasonable negotiation — companies have invested too much in the hiring process to withdraw over a counter. The fear of rescission is systematically miscalibrated.
industry_realities.implication: International students should negotiate with the same assertiveness as domestic candidates. The sponsorship relationship is not made fragile by a salary counter; it is made fragile by poor performance or bad cultural fit.

industry_realities.common_belief: H-1B holders are legally locked in to their employer and cannot credibly threaten to leave
industry_realities.actual_reality: H-1B portability rules allow you to switch employers without waiting for approval, as long as the new petition is filed within 60 days of the switch. You are not trapped.
industry_realities.implication: Knowing and internalizing portability rights converts a perceived permanent constraint into a manageable one — and makes competing offer leverage genuinely credible for H-1B holders.

industry_realities.common_belief: Visa status is purely a negotiating liability — it makes you a more expensive, riskier hire
industry_realities.actual_reality: Visa holders have a demonstrable retention advantage — they are significantly less likely to job-hop due to immigration switching costs, meaning employers get a longer-tenure employee for the same investment
industry_realities.implication: Visa holders should explicitly surface retention value as a counter-narrative to the cost framing, reorienting the conversation from 'I cost more because of sponsorship' to 'I stay longer and that has real value.'

industry_realities.common_belief: If a company says they will sponsor H-1B, the immigration component of the offer is settled
industry_realities.actual_reality: Sponsorship willingness and execution capacity are entirely different things. A company without immigration counsel, prior H-1B filings, or current H-1B employees may be genuinely willing but structurally unable to deliver.
industry_realities.implication: Verify execution infrastructure — not just intent — before signing. Ask about immigration counsel, prior H-1B history, and current H-1B employees as due diligence items equivalent to verifying salary.

industry_realities.common_belief: Under the H-1B lottery, salary negotiation and immigration strategy are separate concerns
industry_realities.actual_reality: Under the post-2025 wage-weighted lottery, negotiating for a higher wage level directly improves lottery odds — salary negotiation and immigration outcomes are structurally linked
industry_realities.implication: Frame salary negotiations in the context of wage level classification explicitly — a higher comp ask is simultaneously an immigration strategy, and both parties benefit from a Level III or IV classification.

industry_realities.common_belief: You need a competing job offer to have real negotiating leverage
industry_realities.actual_reality: A BATNA can be any credible alternative you would genuinely pursue — another degree (resetting OPT clock), CPT positions, or another country's job market. It does not have to be a competing offer.
industry_realities.implication: International students who believe they have no leverage because they lack a competing offer may still have real alternatives they haven't articulated. Identify and document your actual BATNA before entering any negotiation.

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to receiving the first offer
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts immediately or counter-proposes a number based on personal need or what feels safe, driven by relief that the offer exists and fear that any friction will cost the sponsorship
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats the first offer as an opening position, researches market comps before responding, and counters with a specific number grounded in levels.fyi and H-1B salary disclosure data
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The difference between accepting and countering compounds to 10–30% salary differential annually, and hundreds of thousands of dollars over a career

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Framing of visa status in negotiations
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats visa dependency as a liability to manage or hide — either over-apologizes for needing sponsorship or stays silent about immigration topics to avoid attention
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Proactively reframes visa status as a retention differentiator and uses it as a negotiating asset — 'I stay longer, which means your investment in sponsoring me has a higher ROI'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Framing determines whether the employer sees sponsorship as a cost to minimize or an investment in a long-tenure employee — the same fact generates opposite leverage depending on who frames it first

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Discloses OPT/sponsorship need early — in cover letters, early applications, or first contact — out of a sense of obligation to be upfront
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Delays disclosure until the company has decided they want to hire, then states it matter-of-factly as a researched, confident choice — 'I've confirmed this company sponsors H-1B, which is part of why I applied'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Early disclosure makes sponsorship cost a filter before qualifications are assessed; delayed disclosure ensures the employer weighs your value before weighing the cost

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Believes H-1B dependency is a near-total lock-in — that switching employers is so complex and risky that it is not a real option, eliminating all implied BATNA credibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Knows H-1B portability rules precisely — 60-day filing window, ability to start immediately — and uses this knowledge to maintain credible optionality in negotiations
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Employers price compensation partly based on perceived switching costs; a candidate who knows they can leave commands more leverage than one who believes they cannot

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats any 'yes we'll sponsor' signal as confirmation that the immigration component is settled, and moves on to evaluating only the salary number
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Distinguishes sponsorship willingness from execution capacity — asks about immigration counsel, prior H-1B history, current H-1B employees, and gets written confirmation before signing
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A company that wants to sponsor but cannot execute is a material risk that looks identical to a capable sponsor in early conversation — only due diligence reveals the difference

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Believes BATNA requires a competing job offer and, lacking one, concludes they have no leverage and must accept whatever is offered
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Recognizes that any credible alternative counts — another degree, CPT, another country's market — and actively builds and articulates BATNA options before entering negotiation
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Perceived optionality determines negotiating posture; a student who identifies real alternatives negotiates with authentic confidence rather than performed confidence

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Integration of immigration and compensation strategy
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats salary negotiation and immigration planning as separate tracks — negotiates comp with HR and handles visa logistics separately with no connection between the two
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Understands that post-2025 wage level classification directly affects H-1B lottery odds and explicitly uses this alignment to make a joint case for higher comp and better immigration outcomes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Using the wage-weighted lottery argument converts a one-sided salary ask into a mutual benefit conversation — structurally more persuasive and less adversarial
